chunk_summary: This section outlines imaging pathways for diagnosing bone infections, emphasizing resource-adapted strategies and culture-negative management in children.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise imaging approach for pediatric bone infections, including plain radiographs, ultrasound, and MRI.
Recognize the importance of clinical re-evaluation and resource-adapted imaging pathways when advanced imaging is unavailable.
Learn appropriate management strategies for culture-negative infections, including when to escalate diagnostics or therapy.
key_facts: Plain radiographs are the first imaging step for focal bone or joint pain but may appear normal in early osteomyelitis.
Ultrasound is preferred for suspected septic arthritis or subperiosteal fluid, especially in the hip or shoulder.
MRI is the gold standard for detecting early marrow edema and defining abscesses, particularly in pelvic, sacroiliac, and spinal infections.
Bone scans are useful for suspected multifocal disease but provide limited anatomic detail.
Culture-negative infections should not lead to automatic antibiotic escalation if clinical improvement is observed.
If fever, pain, or inflammatory markers persist, broaden the differential to include TB, fungi, and malignancy.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Plain radiographs are the first imaging step in any child with focal bone or joint pain because they help exclude fracture, dislocation, tumor, and late bony destruction. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Imaging strategies for pediatric bone infections
